content:
The first step in writing a compare-and-contrast essay is to choose two topics that are different enough to be compared. For example, compare apples and oranges or winter and summer. Once you've chosen your topics, decide what points of comparison you will focus on. This will help you determine what information to include in your essay.

You need at least three paragraphs in your body, but you can add more if you think they will help your argument. Each body paragraph should focus on one point of comparison which would apply to the subject-by-subject structure. For example, if you're comparing apples and oranges, you might discuss the taste of each fruit, the nutritional value of each fruit, or the cost of each fruit.

Make sure to use transitional words and phrases (such as "in addition," "however," "on the other hand," etc.) throughout your essay so that it flows smoothly from one point to the next.

Conclude your essay by restating your thesis statement and showing how your comparisons support it.Compare refers to both similarities and differences. Contrast only refers to similarities.(Source: PEDIAALinks to an external site.)
